description: The fundamental unit of a transformer's computation is the residual unit. This unit is just a number, often called an activation. As the model computes, this activation is repeatedly updated; the goal of the transformer is to use these residual units in a way that produces reliable predictions.

These units behave like oscillators, reaching their final destination through a series of oscillatory movements. These oscillations become clearer when we graph the change in activation (velocity) against the activation itself.

To fully isolate these oscillations, we can graph acceleration against velocity. Here, we find that residual unit oscillations have a set of canonical rhythms. A unit will often form a full loop on this graph every 2 layers, which is what each color corresponds to. You will also see units go through half, one-and-a-half, or two oscillations over a 2-layer span, with deviations being especially common at the first and final layers.

Modern transformers have thousands of these units, and together, they constitute the model's medium of communication. A transformer contains dozens of distant computational modules, each performing its own unique computation. The only way for them to pass information to one another is through these residual units.

Communication through an oscillatory medium is not unique to transformers. The brain is itself an oscillatory medium, and distant brain regions are known to communicate through oscillatory relationships; specifically, phase and frequency relationships are how the brain coordinates computation and establishes coherence across distant neuronal populations.1 Little research has been done on the oscillatory dynamics of residual units, so it is unknown how phase and frequency contribute to the model's computation. What is evident, however, is that human and machine intelligence utilize an eerily similar communicative medium, and this is perhaps the least glaring similarity between the two.

At the core of human intelligence, we find the hippocampus. The hippocampus, or its functional equivalent, can be found in all vertebrates, where it performs a very simple task: receive information from the rest of the brain, store it in a relational map, and then, when the time comes, push that information back to the brain. The hippocampus is not only what allows us to remember, but what allows us to update our behavior based on the recent or distant past. It is what turns our rigid, pattern-bound cortex into an adaptable, imaginative, and self-directed, engine of possibility.

Much of what makes the hippocampus special is its deepest region: CA3. This is where your bank of memories lives; memories are stored in association with one another, allowing them to be combined, shuffled, and distorted in novel ways (our dreams' eerie balance of reality and fantasy likely emerge from here). This region uses a unique type of network called an associative network, and in 1982, John Hopfield built a computational model of this network.

Below you'll find an interactive Hopfield network. A handful of patterns are baked into its weights as stable states; any partial or corrupted cue draws the network back toward the nearest stored memory. Select any of the stored patterns, then press recall to watch the network remember. You can also store your own drawings as new memories, and the network will learn to recall them.

The modern Hopfield network is mathematically equivalent to the transformer's attention layer.2 Much like the hippocampus, the attention layer is the heart of an LLM's intelligence, allowing it to rapidly update its behavior as new information comes in. This works by having incoming information "attend" to all of the information that came before it. What comes out of this computation is a representation of incoming information that incorporates everything the model has already seen. In other words, the model is modifying its understanding of the present based on the memories the present moment is triggering.

In both systems, the central memory operation sits at the center of an input and output pathway. The active representation passes through the input pathway, which translates the representation into a form better-suited for storage and recall. The translated representation is then processed by the memory unit, which matches the representation to the memories most closely associated with it. Once these associations are made, the output pathway determines how the associations should inform the active representation. This loop repeats throughout the entire computation: once per layer in the transformer, and once per theta cycle in the hippocampus

On the other side of this loop we find a component called the <abbr title="Multilayer Perceptron">MLP</abbr>, short for multilayer perceptron. It maps, roughly, onto the role cortex plays in the hippocampus-cortex loop. While the hippocampus is what makes cognition flexible, cortex is what makes it consistent, and the same is true for the <abbr title="Multilayer Perceptron">MLP</abbr>. The <abbr title="Multilayer Perceptron">MLP</abbr> is where the model holds facts about the world, like the capital of Turkey, or the chemical composition of the atmosphere. It is still, in a sense, memory, but it is a memory of patterns the model has seen consistently across its training data, rather than a memory of recent one-off occurences.

A transformer is a sequence of <abbr title="Multilayer Perceptron">MLP</abbr> and attention layers, each modifying the active representation. Unlike the hippocampus-cortex loop in the brain, there are multiple attention and <abbr title="Multilayer Perceptron">MLP</abbr> layers, so instead of the active representation going through a few components over and over again, it goes through each component once. Moreover, these modules do not modify the residual units directly; instead, they receive the full set of residual units, perform a computation based on those units, then use that computation to either add to or subtract from each residual unit.

Since the invention of the transformer in 2017, a lot of work has gone into making these models more efficient, revealing problems and solutions that mirror those we find in the brain. One example is <abbr title="Key-Value">KV</abbr> Caching. K and V refer to keys and values, which are a core part of the attention computation; keys are analogous to the memories stored in the CA3 component of the hippocampus, whereas values are analogous to the information stored in the output pathway — mainly CA1. In early transformers, keys and values were recomputed every time they were used, which is akin to reconstructing CA3 and CA1 every time you need to recall a memory. Practitioners quickly realized that they could simply compute keys and values once, store them, then reuse them for every subsequent computation. This is where the purely computational attention module acquired the additional responsibility of storing the past.

Additionally, the standard transformer uses multiple attention modules per-layer, rather than just one. Each of these are called an attention head, and the standard attention scheme is referred to as Multi-Head Attention (MHA). Having multiple attention heads is like having multiple hippocampi, each specialized for a specific type of relationship between incoming cues and the store of memories. Researchers realized that a lot of these heads were redundant. More specifically, they realized that the model didn't need that many unique sets of keys and values. This resulted in a multitude of innovations to the attention module, with Multi-Query Attention (MQA) being the most memory-efficient. MQA uses only one set of keys and values, relying on flexible memory cuing to make up for the reduction in specialization. Put another way, MQA is the realization that we only need one CA3, and one CA1, with duplicates only making sense when memory-efficiency is not a significant constraint.

What ended up striking the best balance between efficiency and performance was Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA). In <abbr title="Multi-head Latent Attention">MLA</abbr>, there are still multiple heads with unique keys and values, but rather than being stored separately, the per-head memory stores are merged into a single compressed representation, and each head reconstructs the keys and values it needs at the moment it uses them. This is close to <abbr title="Multi-Query Attention">MQA</abbr>'s centralized storage, yet the heads remain as distinct as they were in full multi-head attention, recovering its performance and often surpassing it.

This is a moment where the machine may be able to teach us something about the brain, which is perhaps the most overlooked by-product of AI research. Advances in neuroscience have given us tremendous insight into the anatomy and activity of the brain, but it is still difficult to determine what computations are actually occurring at any given region. For example, there is a long-standing debate about whether the hippocampus stores memories at all, or merely holds a compact pointer from which a full memory can be reconstructed. MLA tells us that a system at the optimal balance of performance and efficiency would, in fact, only store compact pointers, relying on other components to reconstruct the content of a memory from this pointer.

In general, the central goal of AI research is to find the most efficient means of producing intelligence, which is the exact same problem the brain evolved to solve. It has become incredibly easy to test potential solutions and measure their impact on intelligence, giving us much needed hints for how Mother Nature solved the intelligence problem.

Multi-head Latent Attention was first implemented by DeepSeek-AI,3 who have consistently been at the frontier of AI architectures, while releasing all of the architectural details to the public. Alongside their improvements to the attention head, they have also made significant modifications to the MLP module. Recall that the MLP reflects the model's cortex, where its knowledge of the world is kept. Cortex, unlike the MLP, is not one uniform mass. Rather, it is a mosaic of specialized regions, each attuned to its own kind of information, and at any given moment only a handful are meaningfully engaged. In DeepSeek's pursuit of efficiency, they designed an MLP of that same character, called a Mixture of Experts (MoE). Once again, we see how improvements to artificial intelligence move these systems closer and closer to the brain.

Each innovation so far has modified a module that communicates through the residual stream. The most recent work changes the medium of communication itself. For years a transformer had a single residual stream carrying all communication between modules. As models scaled, that single channel became a bottleneck, and the fix was to split the residual stream into several parallel streams with learned routing between them. These are called hyper-connections.45 We saw earlier that little is known about whether phase and frequency carry meaning in a single stream. With several streams now in play, that question only deepens, and it is here, at the level of the communicative medium, that the mechanisms of intelligence in both brain and machine are least understood.

Although we are clearly very different from the machines we have created, we underestimate the degree to which we have recreated ourselves. If we look behind all of the computational and mathematical formalisms that obscure the underlying nature of AI, we find a set of mechanisms remarkably similar to those employed by the human brain. There was a time to debate whether these machines were truly intelligent and that time has passed; if they are not intelligent, then neither are we.

The issue we must now face is a societal one. Massive sums of money continue to flow into AI development, producing some of the most popular and powerful tools humans have ever known. CEOs warn of mass unemployment, essentially telling the world, and their investors, that they are hoping to consolidate vast portions of economic power. Many have come to fear and fantasize about the permanent underclass that AI advancement will produce, as they rush to exploit their fellow man before time is up. Those of us outside the tech bubble assume they know something we don't, leaving us no other option but to believe what they say.

This has made AI literacy essential to the maintenance of our society. If the general public remains ignorant about how these models work, panic will overwhelm us long before AI ever could. My goal in creating this is to provide a clearer understanding of how modern AI works, and why it works. These machines are not something to reject, nor are they something to worship. They should simply be understood.
